items.q: Why are there no volcanoes at conservative plate boundaries?
items.correct: Conservative boundaries involve plates sliding past each other horizontally — no crust is created or destroyed, so there is no subduction to generate magma.
items.misconception: There are no volcanoes because the boundaries are too cold.
items.random_wrong: There are volcanoes at conservative boundaries — that's where most of them form.
items.why: Volcanism requires magma generation, which needs either subduction or mantle upwelling. Conservative boundaries (like the San Andreas) lack both, but still produce major earthquakes.

items.q: What are the three cells of global atmospheric circulation?
items.correct: The Hadley cell (equator to 30 degrees), Ferrel cell (30 to 60 degrees) and Polar cell (60 degrees to poles) together redistribute heat from equator to poles.
items.misconception: There is one big Hadley cell from the equator to each pole.
items.random_wrong: The three cells are tropical, temperate and arctic, named after climate zones.
items.why: Students often learn 'Hadley' as the only cell. There are three in each hemisphere, and their boundaries (at 30 and 60 degrees) explain global pressure belts, deserts and the polar front.

items.q: What is the 'race to the bottom' critique of globalisation?
items.correct: The argument that TNCs play countries off against each other, relocating to those with lowest wages, weakest regulation and lowest taxes — pressuring governments to cut standards to attract investment.
items.misconception: The race to the bottom means TNCs compete with each other for the lowest prices.
items.random_wrong: Race to the bottom is when companies offshore work to mines and underground factories.
items.why: It's about regulatory competition between states, not price competition between firms. It explains why LICs may keep labour and environmental rules low — competing for FDI rather than improving workers' conditions.
It's Height of land. “Rivers” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Steep slope. “Flat ground” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: On a 1:25,000 OS map, 1 cm = how many metres?
items.why: Exact answer: 250. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
